content.text: Azure Arc-enabled SQL Managed Instance deploys a SQL Server engine inside a Kubernetes cluster on any infrastructure — on-premises, edge, or another cloud. The Arc Data Controller, which runs in the same cluster, handles lifecycle, metrics, log upload, and billing reconciliation with Azure. SQL MI inherits full SQL Server compatibility (built from the same binaries as PaaS SQL MI) while running on hardware you own.

attrs.alt: Arc SQL MI architecture — Data Controller, GP vs BC service tiers, Kubernetes services, Azure ARM

content.text: Arc SQL MI is not a distributed database. Each instance is a single SQL Server pod (GP) or an Availability Group across pods (BC). Data resides on Kubernetes PVCs — size them correctly before deployment, because live expansion requires a storage class that supports volume expansion.
The Data Controller is the prerequisite for any Arc data service. It runs in its own namespace and manages the sqlmi operator, logsdb (Elasticsearch), and metricsdb (InfluxDB).

# 1. Create the data controller namespace
kubectl create namespace arc-data-services

# 2. Create a profile (kubeadm, aks, eks, gke, openshift …)
az arcdata dc config init   --source azure-arc-kubeadm   --path ./arc-dc-config

# 3. Edit ./arc-dc-config/control.json to set:
#    spec.storage.logs.className, spec.storage.data.className,
#    spec.storage.backups.className (StorageClass names from your cluster)
#    spec.settings.azure.subscription, resourceGroup, location

# 4. Deploy (indirect connectivity — no direct ARM access from cluster)
az arcdata dc create   --path ./arc-dc-config   --custom-location myCustomLocation   --name arc-dc01   --subscription $SUBSCRIPTION   --resource-group $RG   --location eastus   --connectivity-mode indirect   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# 5. Monitor rollout
kubectl get pods -n arc-data-services --watch

# General Purpose (1 replica, ReadWriteMany storage)
az sql mi-arc create   --name sqlmi-gp01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --tier GeneralPurpose   --cores-request 2   --cores-limit 4   --memory-request 4Gi   --memory-limit 8Gi   --storage-class-data managed-premium   --storage-class-logs managed-premium   --storage-class-backups managed-premium   --volume-size-data 100Gi   --volume-size-logs 10Gi   --volume-size-backups 100Gi

# Business Critical (3 replicas, ReadWriteOnce)
az sql mi-arc create   --name sqlmi-bc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --tier BusinessCritical   --replicas 3   --cores-request 4   --cores-limit 8   --memory-request 16Gi   --memory-limit 32Gi   --storage-class-data local-storage   --storage-class-logs local-storage   --storage-class-backups nfs-csi   --volume-size-data 500Gi   --volume-size-logs 20Gi   --volume-size-backups 500Gi

# Check endpoint (TDS and controller ports)
az sql mi-arc show --name sqlmi-gp01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --query "status.endpoints"

Arc SQL MI supports automated point-in-time restores using a shared backup PVC. Full and transaction-log backups run automatically; the default retention is 7 days. Restores are performed via the az sql midb-arc CLI, not SSMS — the controller applies the restore plan inside the cluster.

# Show configured retention and backup location
az sql mi-arc show --name sqlmi-gp01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --query "{retention:spec.backup.retentionPeriodInDays, backupPVC:status.highAvailability}"

# Change retention period
az sql mi-arc update --name sqlmi-gp01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --retention-days 14

# Point-in-time restore to a new instance
az sql midb-arc restore   --managed-instance sqlmi-gp01   --name mydb   --dest-name sqlmi-gp01-restore   --time "2024-03-15T10:30:00Z"   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# List available restore times for a database
az sql midb-arc list-restore-windows   --managed-instance sqlmi-gp01   --database-name mydb   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s
ReadWriteMany PVC on GP with a RWO storage class
Use NFS-backed or CSI RWX storage class for GP
Insufficient CPU/memory requests vs node capacity
Set --cores-request to what nodes can actually schedule
az sql mi-arc create fails: data controller not found
Deploy and wait for DC pods to be Running before SQL MI
Metrics / billing not appearing in Azure portal
Match connectivity-mode on DC with what network policy allows (indirect = air-gapped OK)
Skipping custom location for direct mode
ARM resource not visible in portal
Create a Custom Location mapped to arc-data-services namespace first

Arc data services support two connectivity modes that determine how the Data Controller communicates with Azure. The mode is set at Data Controller creation and cannot be changed afterwards — choose carefully.
Upload via az arcdata dc upload (scheduled export)
Real-time ARM projection via custom location
Full CRUD from Azure portal and ARM APIs
Outbound HTTPS from admin workstation only
Yes — maps Arc K8s cluster namespace to ARM

# --- INDIRECT MODE ---
# Export usage data (run from admin workstation with Azure access)
az arcdata dc export   --type usage   --path ./usage-data.json   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# Upload exported usage for billing
az arcdata dc upload --path ./usage-data.json

# Export + upload metrics for Azure Monitor
az arcdata dc export --type metrics --path ./metrics.json   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services --use-k8s
az arcdata dc upload --path ./metrics.json

# --- DIRECT MODE pre-requisites ---
# 1. Arc-connected Kubernetes cluster must exist
# 2. Create Custom Location mapped to arc-data-services namespace
ARC_CLUSTER_ID=$(az connectedk8s show -n myCluster -g $RG --query id -o tsv)

az customlocation create   --resource-group $RG   --name arc-data-cl   --host-resource-id $ARC_CLUSTER_ID   --namespace arc-data-services   --cluster-extension-ids     $(az k8s-extension show -c myCluster -g $RG --cluster-type connectedClusters       --name arc-data-services --query id -o tsv)

# Create DC in direct mode (portal-visible immediately)
az arcdata dc create   --path ./arc-dc-config   --custom-location arc-data-cl   --name arc-dc01   --connectivity-mode direct   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services
High Availability in Business Critical Tier

Business Critical SQL MI deploys an Always On Availability Group across Kubernetes pods using the contained AG (no Windows Server Failover Cluster required). Paxos quorum drives automatic failover — the primary pod's StatefulSet leadership transfers in 20–30 seconds when the primary becomes unresponsive.

# Show current AG state and replica roles
az sql mi-arc show   --name sqlmi-bc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --query "status.highAvailability"

# Output example:
# {
#   "healthState": "Healthy",
#   "mirroringCertExpiry": "2025-03-01T00:00:00Z",
#   "primaryReplicaInternalDnsName": "sqlmi-bc01-0.sqlmi-bc01-p.arc-data-services.svc",
#   "secondaryReplicasInternalDnsNames": [
#     "sqlmi-bc01-1.sqlmi-bc01-p.arc-data-services.svc",
#     "sqlmi-bc01-2.sqlmi-bc01-p.arc-data-services.svc"
#   ]
# }

# Check pod-level readiness (the secondary-external LB serves read workloads)
kubectl get pods -n arc-data-services -l app=sqlmi-bc01

# Force manual failover (for maintenance of primary node)
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-bc01-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd   -S localhost,1433   -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "ALTER AVAILABILITY GROUP [containedAG] FAILOVER"

# Reconnect: the primary-external LoadBalancer endpoint auto-follows the new primary
# No connection string change needed — DNS resolves to the current primary service

# Scale replicas (BC only, 2–5)
az sql mi-arc update   --name sqlmi-bc01   --replicas 5   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

The Arc Data Controller ships a metrics stack (InfluxDB + Grafana) and a logs stack (Elasticsearch + Kibana) that run inside the cluster. In indirect mode these are local only; in direct mode the controller forwards them to Azure Monitor and Log Analytics.

# Access the local Grafana dashboard (indirect mode)
az arcdata dc endpoint list   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# Port-forward Grafana if no external LB
kubectl port-forward svc/metricsui-external-svc 3000:3000 -n arc-data-services &
# Open http://localhost:3000 (default creds from DC secret 'metricsui-secret')

# Port-forward Kibana
kubectl port-forward svc/logsui-external-svc 5601:5601 -n arc-data-services &

# In direct mode — push metrics to Azure Monitor (triggered by controller automatically)
# Verify the MSI assigned to the controller has "Monitoring Metrics Publisher" on the LA workspace
az role assignment create   --assignee "$(az arcdata dc show -n arc-dc01 -g $RG --query identity.principalId -o tsv)"   --role "Monitoring Metrics Publisher"   --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/$WORKSPACE"

# Query SQL MI wait stats from Log Analytics (direct mode, after upload)
# AzureDiagnostics
# | where Category == "SQLInsights"
# | where ResourceType == "SQLMANAGEDINSTANCES"
# | where MetricName == "waiting_tasks_count"
# | summarize avg(Total) by bin(TimeGenerated, 5m), ResourceName
# | render timechart

# Check Elasticsearch index health in indirect mode
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services   $(kubectl get pod -n arc-data-services -l app=logsdb -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')   -- curl -s http://localhost:9200/_cluster/health | python3 -m json.tool

Arc SQL MI upgrades are performed by updating the desired version on the Data Controller, which then orchestrates a rolling upgrade of SQL MI pods. The Data Controller itself must be upgraded first — SQL MI can never run a version newer than the DC.

# Show available versions
az arcdata dc list-upgrades --k8s-namespace arc-data-services --use-k8s

# Upgrade the Data Controller first
az arcdata dc upgrade   --desired-version v1.29.0_2024-03-12   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# Wait for DC upgrade to complete
kubectl rollout status deployment/arc-dc-control -n arc-data-services

# Upgrade SQL MI (GP: brief downtime; BC: rolling, zero downtime)
az sql mi-arc upgrade   --name sqlmi-gp01   --desired-version v1.29.0_2024-03-12   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# Disable auto-upgrade (pin to current version for change-control environments)
az sql mi-arc update   --name sqlmi-gp01   --auto-upgrade-desired-version false   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# Verify post-upgrade SQL Server version
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-gp01-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "SELECT @@VERSION"
Security: TLS, Microsoft Entra ID, and Transparent Data Encryption

Arc SQL MI auto-generates a self-signed TLS certificate at deploy time. For production, supply a CA-signed certificate. Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) authentication requires direct connectivity mode — the controller forwards token validation requests to Entra. TDE is available at the database level using the same mechanism as SQL Server on-premises.

# Supply a custom TLS certificate (PEM-encoded cert + key)
kubectl create secret generic sqlmi-gp01-certificate   --from-file=certificate.pem=./my-cert.pem   --from-file=privatekey.pem=./my-key.pem   -n arc-data-services

az sql mi-arc update   --name sqlmi-gp01   --service-cert-secret sqlmi-gp01-certificate   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# Enable Microsoft Entra ID authentication (direct mode only)
# Create the AD connector first
az arcdata ad-connector create   --name arcadconnector   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --realm "CONTOSO.COM"   --nameserver-addresses "192.168.1.10"   --account "svc-arc-sql"   --keytab-secret arcadconnector-keytab

# Then bind the SQL MI to the AD connector
az sql mi-arc update   --name sqlmi-gp01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --ad-connector-name arcadconnector   --ad-account-name sqlmi-svc-account

# Enable TDE on a database (T-SQL, works the same as SQL Server on-prem)
# CREATE MASTER KEY ENCRYPTION BY PASSWORD = 'StrongPassword1!';
# CREATE CERTIFICATE TDECert WITH SUBJECT = 'TDE Certificate';
# CREATE DATABASE ENCRYPTION KEY WITH ALGORITHM = AES_256
#   ENCRYPTION BY SERVER CERTIFICATE TDECert;
# ALTER DATABASE mydb SET ENCRYPTION ON;

# Verify TDE state
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-gp01-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "SELECT db_name(database_id), encryption_state FROM sys.dm_database_encryption_keys"
Resource Graph Queries for Arc SQL MI

// All Arc SQL MI instances with tier and core counts
resources
| where type == "microsoft.azurearcdata/sqlmanagedinstances"
| extend tier = properties.spec.tier
| extend cores = properties.spec.scheduling.default.resources.limits["cpu"]
| extend memory = properties.spec.scheduling.default.resources.limits["memory"]
| extend location = location
| project name, resourceGroup, tier, cores, memory, location

// Instances not recently synced (may be disconnected DCs)
resources
| where type == "microsoft.azurearcdata/sqlmanagedinstances"
| extend lastUploadTime = todatetime(properties.status.lastUpdateTime)
| where lastUploadTime < ago(2h)
| project name, resourceGroup, lastUploadTime

// Arc SQL MIs without Defender for SQL enabled
resources
| where type == "microsoft.azurearcdata/sqlmanagedinstances"
| where isnull(properties.spec.security.adminLoginSecret)
| project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId
Migrating On-Premises Databases to Arc SQL MI

Arc SQL MI supports all SQL Server migration paths that work with SQL Server on Linux. The recommended approach uses Azure Database Migration Service (DMS) for large databases or backup/restore for smaller ones. Because Arc SQL MI runs the same SQL Server engine, most on-premises databases migrate without schema changes.

# ---- Backup/Restore Migration ----
# 1. Take backup on source (on-prem SQL Server)
# BACKUP DATABASE mydb TO DISK = N'/backup/mydb_full.bak'
# WITH NOFORMAT, INIT, NAME = N'mydb-Full', COMPRESSION;

# 2. Copy backup file to a PVC accessible from the Arc SQL MI pod
# Option A: use kubectl cp (for dev/test)
kubectl cp ./mydb_full.bak arc-data-services/sqlmi-gp01-0:/var/opt/mssql/backup/mydb_full.bak

# Option B: mount a shared NFS PVC as backup volume (production)
# Already configured via --storage-class-backups on deployment

# 3. Restore inside the pod
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-gp01-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd   -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "RESTORE DATABASE [mydb] FROM DISK = N'/var/opt/mssql/backup/mydb_full.bak'       WITH MOVE 'mydb' TO '/var/opt/mssql/data/mydb.mdf',            MOVE 'mydb_log' TO '/var/opt/mssql/data/mydb_log.ldf', REPLACE"

# ---- DMS migration for large databases ----
# DMS supports online migration (minimal downtime) via log shipping

# Create DMS project (source: on-prem SQL Server, target: Arc SQL MI primary endpoint)
az dms project create   --name arcmig   --resource-group $RG   --service-name $DMS_SVC   --source-platform SQL   --target-platform SQLMI

# The target connection uses the primary-external LoadBalancer IP of the Arc SQL MI
# --target-connection-json '{"serverName":"<EXTERNAL_IP>","port":1433,"auth":"sqlAuthentication","userName":"sa","password":"..."}'

# ---- Verify migration ----
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-gp01-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "SELECT name, state_desc, compatibility_level FROM sys.databases WHERE name='mydb'"

Arc SQL MI CPU and memory limits map directly to Kubernetes resource limits on the SQL Server container. Unlike PaaS SQL MI, there is no automatic resource tier — you control vCore and memory limits explicitly, and the SQL Server optimizer calibrates max server memory and degree of parallelism from the container limits at startup.
MAXDOP=4, instant file init via privileged pod
BC tier for HA; pin pods to high-IOPS nodes

# Adjust resource limits on running instance (triggers rolling restart on GP)
az sql mi-arc update   --name sqlmi-gp01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --cores-limit 16   --memory-limit 64Gi

# Check current resource allocation from kubectl
kubectl get pod sqlmi-gp01-0 -n arc-data-services   -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[?(@.name=="arc-sqlmi")].resources}' | python3 -m json.tool

# SQL Server max server memory is auto-set to ~90% of container memory limit.
# Verify inside the instance:
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-gp01-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "SELECT value_in_use FROM sys.configurations WHERE name='max server memory (MB)'"

# Monitor wait stats to identify bottlenecks
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-gp01-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "SELECT TOP 10 wait_type, waiting_tasks_count, wait_time_ms/waiting_tasks_count AS avg_wait_ms
      FROM sys.dm_os_wait_stats
      WHERE waiting_tasks_count > 0
        AND wait_type NOT IN ('SLEEP_TASK','BROKER_TO_FLUSH','CHECKPOINT_QUEUE',
                              'REQUEST_FOR_DEADLOCK_SEARCH','XE_TIMER_EVENT',
                              'LOGMGR_QUEUE','DIRTY_PAGE_POLL')
      ORDER BY wait_time_ms DESC"

# Check storage I/O latency (critical for GP on NFS)
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-gp01-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "SELECT DB_NAME(database_id) AS db_name,
             io_stall_read_ms / NULLIF(num_of_reads,0) AS avg_read_ms,
             io_stall_write_ms / NULLIF(num_of_writes,0) AS avg_write_ms
      FROM sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats(NULL,NULL)
      WHERE num_of_reads > 0 OR num_of_writes > 0
      ORDER BY avg_write_ms DESC"

For cross-site disaster recovery, Arc SQL MI Business Critical supports distributed availability groups (DAGs) between two Arc-managed instances — even in different Kubernetes clusters. The DAG secondary is a warm standby that can be promoted to primary during a site failure.

DAGs between Arc SQL MI instances are not the same as linking to Azure SQL MI in the cloud. That feature (Managed Instance Link) is a separate capability for hybrid replication to PaaS SQL MI and is not covered here.

# Pre-requisite: two BC SQL MI instances in separate clusters, each with an external LB
# PRIMARY: sqlmi-bc-east (cluster-east), SECONDARY: sqlmi-bc-west (cluster-west)

# 1. Export the mirroring certificate from the primary
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-bc-east-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "BACKUP CERTIFICATE sqlmi_bc_east_cert TO FILE = '/tmp/east_cert.cer'
      WITH PRIVATE KEY (FILE = '/tmp/east_cert.pvk', ENCRYPTION BY PASSWORD = 'CertPass1!')"

kubectl cp arc-data-services/sqlmi-bc-east-0:/tmp/east_cert.cer ./east_cert.cer
kubectl cp arc-data-services/sqlmi-bc-east-0:/tmp/east_cert.pvk ./east_cert.pvk

# 2. Copy and import cert on secondary cluster
kubectl cp ./east_cert.cer arc-data-services-west/sqlmi-bc-west-0:/tmp/east_cert.cer
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services-west sqlmi-bc-west-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "CREATE CERTIFICATE east_mirror_cert FROM FILE = '/tmp/east_cert.cer'"

# 3. Create DAG on primary (T-SQL)
# CREATE AVAILABILITY GROUP [dag-cross-site]
#   WITH (DISTRIBUTED)
#   AVAILABILITY GROUP ON
#     'containedAG' WITH (
#       LISTENER_URL = 'TCP://<primary-external-ip>:5022',
#       AVAILABILITY_MODE = ASYNCHRONOUS_COMMIT,
#       FAILOVER_MODE = MANUAL,
#       SEEDING_MODE = AUTOMATIC),
#     'containedAG' WITH (
#       LISTENER_URL = 'TCP://<secondary-external-ip>:5022',
#       AVAILABILITY_MODE = ASYNCHRONOUS_COMMIT,
#       FAILOVER_MODE = MANUAL,
#       SEEDING_MODE = AUTOMATIC);

# 4. Join secondary AG to the DAG (run on secondary cluster pod)
# ALTER AVAILABILITY GROUP [dag-cross-site] JOIN
#   AVAILABILITY GROUP ON
#     'containedAG' WITH (
#       LISTENER_URL = 'TCP://<secondary-external-ip>:5022',
#       AVAILABILITY_MODE = ASYNCHRONOUS_COMMIT,
#       FAILOVER_MODE = MANUAL,
#       SEEDING_MODE = AUTOMATIC);

# 5. Check DAG sync state
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services sqlmi-bc-east-0 --   /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost,1433 -U sa -P "$SA_PASSWORD"   -Q "SELECT ag.name, ars.role_desc, ars.synchronization_health_desc, ars.last_commit_time
      FROM sys.dm_hadr_availability_replica_states ars
      JOIN sys.availability_groups ag ON ars.group_id = ag.group_id"

Arc SQL Managed Instance extends the Azure SQL MI feature set to Kubernetes clusters anywhere. Choose General Purpose for cost-sensitive workloads with RWX storage and short planned maintenance windows, or Business Critical for sub-30-second failover with an AG-based replica set. The Arc Data Controller is always the prerequisite — it manages operator lifecycle, uploads metrics and logs, and provides the PITR backup infrastructure. Size PVCs generously before deployment, because live resizing depends on the storage class, not the SQL MI operator.
